Apple_Black_rot: A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. It features circular spots with light brown centers and distinct purple margins resembling frog eyes.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. The lesions are scattered irregularly across the entire leaf surface.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. Early symptoms appear as tiny purple specks visible on the upper leaf surface.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. In late stages, the centers of the spots turn gray or tan and may crack.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. The spots often contain concentric rings giving them a target-like appearance.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. Tiny black fungal fruiting bodies (pycnidia) are visible in the center of mature lesions.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. The lesions are clearly delimited from the healthy green tissue by a sharp border.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. The distribution of spots is random, affecting both young and old leaves.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. Severe infection leads to chlorosis or yellowing surrounding the frog-eye spots.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. The spots remain discrete and circular rather than expanding into large blights.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. The infected tissue feels papery and brittle to the touch.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. Lesions may enlarge asymmetrically but generally maintain a round shape.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. The spots are typically 2 to 5 mm in diameter.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. It is distinguished by the reddish-purple halo surrounding the necrotic center.
A photo of an apple leaf with black rot. The spots are isolated and do not typically follow the leaf veins.
Apple_Cedar_rust: A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. It displays bright orange or yellow circular spots on the upper leaf surface.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. The spots are bordered by a distinct red or rust-colored ring.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. On the underside, tube-like projections or fringes are clustered directly beneath the upper spots.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. The lesions are often slightly thickened or raised compared to the surrounding tissue.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. Tiny black dots (spermogonia) appear in the center of the orange lesions on the upper side.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. The distribution is often grouped, with multiple spots appearing on a single leaf.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. The spots are typically located randomly on the leaf blade, not restricted by veins.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. Heavy infection causes the leaf to curl downwards or drop prematurely.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. The tubular structures on the lower surface have a fringed or hairy appearance.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. The lesions are opaque and block light, creating a stark contrast with green tissue.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. Early symptoms are small pale yellow spots that rapidly turn orange.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. The diameter of the lesions can range from small dots to 1 cm wide.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. A yellow halo separates the orange center from the green leaf tissue.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. The texture of the spot is swollen and fleshy.
A photo of an apple leaf with cedar rust. It presents a dual-sided symptom profile: flat orange top, spiky bottom.
Apple_Healthy: A photo of a healthy apple leaf. It has a vibrant, uniform green color across the entire surface.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. The texture is smooth and slightly waxy.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. The margins are finely serrated with no signs of browning or necrosis.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. The veins are distinct, pale green, and structurally sound.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. There are no spots, holes, or discolorations on the blade.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. The leaf is flat and fully expanded, not curled or crinkled.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. The underside is a lighter, silvery-green color but free of mold.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. The tip of the leaf is pointed and intact.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. The petiole is strong and connects firmly to the stem.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. It reflects light evenly, indicating a healthy cuticle.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. There is no evidence of insect feeding or stippling.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. The leaf arrangement is alternate, but the individual leaf is simple.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. It shows vigorous growth without stunting.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. The leaf surface is free from any powdery or fuzzy residue.
A photo of a healthy apple leaf. It has an ovate shape with a rounded base.

A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. The margins of the lesions are feathery and undefined.

A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. Lesions are frequently found along the main veins of the leaf.

A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. As the disease progresses, the spots turn brown and necrotic.
A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. The distribution can be irregular, affecting both the upper and lower surfaces.
A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. Severe infection leads to yellowing (chlorosis) and premature leaf drop.

A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. Older lesions may become raised and corky.

A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. The infected tissue eventually dries out and may tear.
A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. It lacks the bright halo seen in other spot diseases.
A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. Young leaves are most susceptible and show the most deformation.
A photo of an apple leaf with apple scab. The spots are often more numerous on the leaf underside in early spring.

A photo of a healthy potato leaf. The leaf is fully expanded and turgid.
A photo of a healthy potato leaf. It shows no yellowing or chlorosis.
A photo of a healthy potato leaf. The tips of the leaflets are pointed.
A photo of a healthy potato leaf. It looks structurally sound.
Potato_Late_blight: A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. It features large, irregular, water-soaked blotches.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. The lesions are pale green initially, turning dark brown or black.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. The spots have no defined borders and spread rapidly.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. White, fuzzy mildew appears on the underside of lesions in humid weather.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. It often starts at the leaf tips or margins.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. The infected tissue looks soft, rotting, or greasy.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. It can cross major veins without restriction.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. The entire leaf shrivels and dies quickly.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. It looks as if the leaf has been scalded or frost-bitten.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. The stems and petioles also turn black and rot.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. It lacks the concentric rings seen in early blight.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. The decay often has a foul odor.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. A pale green border often surrounds the dark necrotic area.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. It thrives in cool, wet conditions.
A photo of a potato leaf with late blight. It is highly destructive and aggressive.
Raspberry_Healthy: A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. It is pinnately compound with 3 to 5 leaflets.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. The margins are doubly serrated and distinct.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. The upper surface is green and has a rugose (wrinkled) texture.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. The underside is often silver or white (tomentose).
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. The veins are deeply impressed on the upper surface.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. Small thorns or prickles may be present on the petiole.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. There are no orange rust pustules.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. It is free from cane blight lesions or spots.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. The leaflets are ovate to broad-ovate.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. New growth is often lighter green and folded.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. It shows no yellowing or mosaic patterns.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. The leaf feels textured and slightly hairy.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. It is turgid and not wilted.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. The tips are pointed.
A photo of a healthy raspberry leaf. It hangs naturally from the cane.
Soybean_Healthy: A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. It is trifoliate with three ovate leaflets.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. The surface is covered in fine hairs (pubescent).
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. The color is a rich, uniform green.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. The margins are entire and smooth.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. It is free from brown pustules or frog-eye spots.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. There is no mosaic mottling or crinkling.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. The veins are distinct and netted.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. It shows no signs of herbicide damage like cupping.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. The texture is soft due to the hairs.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. The leaflets are attached to a long petiole.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. It looks vigorous and structurally sound.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. There is no marginal necrosis.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. The leaf blade is flat.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. It is free from insect holes.
A photo of a healthy soybean leaf. The color is consistent across the leaflet.
Squash_Powdery_mildew: A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. It is covered in white, powdery fungal spots.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. The white spots look like splashes of paint or talcum powder.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. It starts as discrete circular colonies.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. The spots merge to cover the entire leaf surface.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. The fungus is superficial and can be rubbed off.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. It is often visible on both upper and lower surfaces.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. The underlying tissue remains green initially but turns yellow later.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. Older infections cause the leaf to turn brown and crispy.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. It can spread to the petiole and stem.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. The leaf may look dull before the white powder appears.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. It reduces photosynthesis significantly.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. The white mat consists of mycelium and spores.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. It does not cause water-soaked spots.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. The distribution is often patchy.
A photo of a squash leaf with powdery mildew. It gives the leaf a frosted appearance.
Strawberry_Healthy: A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. It is trifoliate with three leaflets.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. The leaflets are obovate or egg-shaped.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. The margins are coarsely serrated with large teeth.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. The surface is shiny and dark green.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. The veins are parallel and closely spaced.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. The petiole is long and often hairy.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. There are no purple, red, or brown spots.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. It grows in a rosette pattern from the crown.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. The leaf texture is firm and slightly pleated.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. It is free from mite distortion or crinkling.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. The tips of the serrations are not brown.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. It shows no signs of scorching.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. The underside is lighter green.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. It looks fresh and turgid.
A photo of a healthy strawberry leaf. The leaflets are attached at a single point.

A photo of a strawberry leaf with leaf scorch. The spots lack the white center seen in common leaf spot.
A photo of a strawberry leaf with leaf scorch. The lesions enlarge and coalesce into large burned areas.
A photo of a strawberry leaf with leaf scorch. It gives the leaf a burnt or scorched appearance.
A photo of a strawberry leaf with leaf scorch. The centers of the spots may turn brown while margins remain purple.
A photo of a strawberry leaf with leaf scorch. It often causes the leaf margins to curl upward and dry out.
A photo of a strawberry leaf with leaf scorch. The distribution can cover the entire leaf surface.

A photo of a strawberry leaf with leaf scorch. It affects both leaves and sepals.

A photo of a strawberry leaf with leaf scorch. Lesions are indeterminate in shape.

Tomato_Bacterial_spot: A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. It features tiny, circular, dark brown to black spots.
A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. The lesions are usually smaller than 3mm.
A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. The spots appear greasy or water-soaked initially.
A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. Lesions are densely scattered over the leaf surface.
A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. A thin yellow halo may surround the spot.
A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. The center of the spot may dry out and tear.
A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. It often concentrates on the leaf margins.

A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. It causes the leaf to turn yellow and drop.

A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. No fungal mycelium is present.
A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. It affects both young and old leaves.
A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. Wetness is required for infection.
A photo of a tomato leaf with bacterial spot. The leaf looks ragged due to the spots.
Tomato_Early_blight: A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. It displays brown circular lesions with concentric rings.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. The 'target-board' pattern is the key identifier.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. A distinct yellow chlorotic halo usually surrounds the lesion.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. It typically starts on the bottom leaves (senescing tissue).
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. The shape of the lesion is oval to angular.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. It progresses upwards through the plant.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. The dead tissue is dark brown and papery.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. It causes significant defoliation of the lower plant.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. The texture of the spot is velvety due to spore production.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. Lesions can enlarge to 1 cm or more.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. It is caused by Alternaria solani.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. The halo is broad and fades into the green.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. Stem cankers with rings may also occur.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. It is distinct from the irregular blotches of late blight.
A photo of a tomato leaf with early blight. The spots are often limited by veins.
Tomato_Healthy: A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. It is an interruptedly pinnate compound leaf.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. The lobes are deep and irregular.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. The color is a uniform medium to dark green.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. The surface is covered in glandular hairs.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. It has a distinct tomato smell when touched.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. The leaflets are flat, not curled or rolled.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. There are no spots, yellow mottles, or holes.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. The texture is soft and fuzzy.

A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. There are no leaf miner trails.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. The stem attachment is succulent and firm.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. It shows vigorous growth.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. The margins are lobed but not necrotic.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. It is fully expanded and turgid.
A photo of a healthy tomato leaf. The underside is lighter green.

A photo of a tomato leaf with late blight. The spots have no defined borders and spread rapidly.
A photo of a tomato leaf with late blight. It destroys the foliage very quickly.
A photo of a tomato leaf with late blight. The entire leaf shrivels, turns brown, and dies.

A photo of a tomato leaf with leaf mold. The spots have indefinite margins.

A photo of a tomato leaf with leaf mold. The yellow spots coalesce to kill the leaf.

A photo of a tomato leaf with leaf mold. It rarely affects the fruit.

A photo of a tomato leaf with leaf mold. The spots are often bounded by veins.
A photo of a tomato leaf with leaf mold. It causes the leaf to wither and turn brown.

A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. The mosaic discoloration is distributed across the leaf blade.

A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. The leaf surface appears bumpy, blistered, or puckered.
A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. There is usually no necrosis or dead spots.
A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. The plant looks stunted and bushy.
A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. New growth is most severely affected.
A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. The pattern is systemic throughout the plant.
A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. It causes uneven ripening of the fruit.

A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. It is mechanically transmitted by touch.
A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. The leaf edge may be wavy.
A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. It is distinct from nutritional chlorosis which is more uniform.
A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. The leaf texture is often brittle.
A photo of a tomato leaf with mosaic virus. Fern-leaf symptoms are a severe form.

A photo of a tomato leaf with Septoria leaf spot. The center of the spot is characteristically gray or white.
A photo of a tomato leaf with Septoria leaf spot. The margin is a distinct dark brown or black ring.
A photo of a tomato leaf with Septoria leaf spot. Tiny black dots (pycnidia) are visible in the white center.

A photo of a tomato leaf with Septoria leaf spot. It does not affect the fruit, only the leaves.

A photo of a tomato leaf with Septoria leaf spot. It is a 'splash dispersed' disease, moving upward.
A photo of a tomato leaf with Septoria leaf spot. The spots are uniform in size.
A photo of a tomato leaf with Septoria leaf spot. It causes sunscald on fruit due to defoliation.
A photo of a tomato leaf with Septoria leaf spot. It is distinct from bacterial spot which lacks the white center.

A photo of a tomato leaf with Septoria leaf spot. It is favored by wet foliage.

A photo of a tomato leaf with spider mites. The stippling makes the leaf look dusty or bleached.
A photo of a tomato leaf with spider mites. The leaf turns bronze, gray, or silvery due to chlorophyll loss.

A photo of a tomato leaf with spider mites. The damage starts as pinprick yellow dots.
A photo of a tomato leaf with spider mites. It weakens the plant significantly.
A photo of a tomato leaf with spider mites. The webbing can bridge between leaves.
A photo of a tomato leaf with spider mites. It is not a fungal disease.
Tomato_Target_Spot: A photo of a tomato leaf with target spot. It features small, necrotic brown lesions.

A photo of a tomato leaf with target spot. It attacks both leaves and fruit.
A photo of a tomato leaf with target spot. The rings are less distinct and tighter than in early blight.
A photo of a tomato leaf with target spot. It causes rapid defoliation starting from the bottom.
A photo of a tomato leaf with target spot. The spots are often zonate.

A photo of a tomato leaf with target spot. The spots are dark brown and necrotic.
A photo of a tomato leaf with target spot. Lesions are numerous and scattered.
A photo of a tomato leaf with target spot. It thrives in warm, humid conditions.
A photo of a tomato leaf with target spot. The tissue around the spot turns yellow.
Tomato_Yellow_leaf_curl_virus: A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. The leaf margins curl upwards strongly and cup.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. It features distinct yellowing between the veins (interveinal chlorosis).
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. The leaves are reduced in size and look crumpled.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. The plant has a stunted, bushy, and erect habit.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. The leaf texture is thickened, rubbery, or leathery.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. There is no necrosis or dead spots usually.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. Flowers often drop off, preventing fruit set.

A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. The veins remain green and may appear thickened.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. New growth is most severely curled.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. It is a geminivirus infection.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. The leaf color is a pale, chlorotic green.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. It does not cause mosaic patterns.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. The curling is upward, unlike some other viruses.
A photo of a tomato leaf with yellow leaf curl virus. The plant stops growing vertically.
